---
title: "Gotta Visualize 'Em All!"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Vincent Sciulli"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/mt_coronet_by_kaihatsuyt_ddc6te7-fullview.jpg #Ref [1] image credit
  data-background-size: stretch
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: Load Packages
#| include: false

# Load packages here
pacman::p_load(tidymodels, tidyverse, readxl, ggrepel)

```

```{r}
#| label: Loading data and general data wrangling
#| include: false
#| warning: false

# Load data here
# Ref [2] for starters, evo_stage, and region data
pokemon <- read_excel("data/pokemon_df_filt.xlsx")

pokemon <- pokemon |>
  
  mutate(

    evo_stage = as.numeric(evo_stage),
    base_stat_total = (attack + defense + special_attack + special_defense + speed + hp)) |>
  
  # Filtering out the variables I will not be using
  select(-c("height", "weight", "species_id", "base_experience", "color_1", "color_2", "color_f", "egg_group_1", "egg_group_2", "url_icon", "url_image"))

```

### Introduction

For my INFO 526 final project, we'll be jumping into the world of Pokemon to answer:

- Which starter is the best, statistically, throughout the evolutionary stages?
- What does the type diversity look like through the various regions of Pokemon?

While answering the above, we'll get some insight on how well the Pokemon developers, GameFreak, balanced the games and pokemon across generations.

## Motivation

This is a passion project as I have been a fan of the series for as long as I remember! The questions I'm answering are ones that "little me" would want to know as I have always been curious on those topics along with game balance in general!

![Turtwig "The Goat" James - Image credit: Ref \[3\]](images/goat.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

## Dataset Peek

```{r}
#| Label: Table Dataset
#| include: false

# Ref [4] for help with establishing the dataframe and table
poke_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("id","pokemon","type_1","type_2","hp","attack","defense","special_attack", "special_defense","speed","generation_id","starter*","evo_stage*", "region*", "base_stat_total*"),
  
  Class = c("integer", "character", "character", "character", "integer", "integer", "integer", "integer", "integer", "integer", "integer", "character", "integer", "character", "integer"),
  
  Description = c("The unique ID of each Pokemon.",
                  "The name of each pokemon.",
                  "The primary type.",
                  "The secondary type.",
                  "The HP (hit points).",
                  "The attack points.",
                  "The defense points.",
                  "The special attack points.",
                  "The special defense points.",
                  "The speed.",
                  "The generation ID of each Pokemon.",
                  "If the Pokemon is a starter or not.",
                  "Which evolutionary stage is the given Pokemon.",
                  "List of region(s) where the given Pokemon is found in the regional Pokedex",
                  "Sum of the hp, attack, defense, speed, special_attack, and special_defense variables")
  
)
```

```{r}
#| Label: Variable Table

# Ref [5] for kable_styling functions
poke_table |> 
  kableExtra::kable() |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 20,
                            full_width = FALSE)
```

\*New variable added to dataset

## 

```{r}
#| Label: Final Project Q1 - Which starter is best?

# Extracting my starters data
starters <- pokemon|>
  filter(starter == "Yes")


starters |>
  
  ggplot(aes(x = generation_id, y = base_stat_total))+
  
  geom_point(aes(color = type_1, shape = type_1))+
  
  facet_wrap(~evo_stage, scales = "free", ncol=1, labeller = labeller(
    evo_stage = c(
      "1" = "Starter",
      "2" = "Mid",
      "3" = "Final"
    )
  )) +
  
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = pokemon), 
                   size = 2.2,
                  nudge.y = 0.1,
                  segment.color = NA,
                  max.overlaps = 3)+ #If nothing is labeled then theyre all there
                
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("triangle", "square", "circle"))+
  
  labs(
    x = "Generation",
    y = "Base Stat Total",
    title = "Base Stat Totals for Starters",
    subtitle = "From Generation 1 to Generation 7",
    color = "Starter Type",
    shape = "Starter Type",
    caption = "Source: 'Pokemon' dataset | TidyTuesday\nOverlapping starters not labeled" #Ref [6] for original dataset
  )+
  
  # Ref [7] for themes
  theme_bw()+
    
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

```

## 

- If we want to look at final evolutions specifically, Swampert looks like our champion! Outside of generations 5 and 7, there is a clear winner with Fire taking 4 out of 5!

- GameFreak has had varying balance ideologies across the generations. Mirrored approach in Gens 1, 2 and 4. Mid stage evolutions 5 out of 7 generations being identical in base stat total. Gen 7 being balanced across evolution stages.

## 

```{r}
#| Label: Final Project Q2 Data Wrangling
#| include: false

pokemon_regions <- pokemon |>
  
  # Wrangling my data so that my types can be counted properly in upcoming steps before splitting regions
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(type_1, type_2),
    names_to = "primary/secondary",
    values_to = "type"
  )   |>
  filter(!type == "NA")|> # Removes any rows of Pokemon that only had one type
  

#Ref [8] for separate_rows() 
  separate_rows(region, sep =",\\s*") |>
  
  group_by(region, type) |> # Group by regions to get our region count and then group by type to get the type count
    summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  
  # Factoring my regions so that they are plotted in order of generation
  mutate(
    region = str_trim(region),
    region = factor(region, levels = c("Kanto", "Johto", "Hoenn", "Sinnoh", "Unova", "Kalos", "Alola"))
  )
  

```

```{r}
#| Label: Final Project Q2 Data Wrangling
#| fig-height: 9 # Adjusting height to fit the seven generations

pokemon_regions |>
  
  ggplot(aes(x = count, y = "", color = type))+
  
  facet_wrap(~region, scales = "free_x", ncol =1)+
  
  #Ref [9] to help with overlap and position_jitter()
  geom_point(shape = "square", size = 3, position = position_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.28, seed = 3)) +
  
  # Label the count number under each dot
  geom_text(aes(label = count), size = 2.8, color = "black", vjust = 3.2, show.legend = FALSE)+
  
  # Labeling
  labs(
    x = "Amount of Pokemon",
    y = NULL,
    title = "Type Diversity across regions",
    subtitle = "Generations 1 through 7",
    color = "Type",
    caption = "Sources: 'Pokemon' dataset | TidyTuesday\nbulbapeida"
  )+
  
  #Ref [10] for color codes
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "#91A119", #Bug
    "#624D4E", #Dark
    "#5060E1", #Dragon
    "#FAC000", #Electric
    "#EF70EF", #Fairy
    "#FF8000", #Fighting
    "#E62829", #Fire
    "#81B9EF", #Flying
    "#704170", #Ghost
    "#3FA129", #Grass
    "#915121", #Ground
    "#3DCEF3", #Ice
    "#9FA19F", #Normal
    "#9141CB", #Poison
    "#EF4179", #Psychic
    "#AFA981", #Rock
    "#60A1B8", #Steel
    "#2980EF" #Water
  ))+
  
  theme_bw() +
  
  theme(
    
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 14),
    
    legend.background = element_rect(color = "black"),
    
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    
    strip.text = element_text(size=10, face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color="black"),
    
    plot.margin = margin(0,0,0,0)
  )
```

## 

- Right off the bat, we can see that Water has taken a clear lead in terms of diversity across the regions with Kanto having one more Poison type Pokemon. Generally, Flying and Normal are 2nd and 3rd.

- Types like Steel, Ice, Ghost and Dragon generally are on the lower end of a region's variety. Sinnoh appears to be the most balanced with all types being relatively close together and has a min/max of 28 where as the other regions are between 31-56 in some cases.

## Conclusion & Takeaways

- GameFreak has not been consistent with balancing the franchise as it went on. Fire type starters are generally the best starter type in terms of total stat points

- Water holds a disproportionate amount of a region's roster across generations which could be the answer to the starter imbalance. Fire is weak to Water so the increase in stats could be to address this. Whereas, Grass is strong against water so Pokemon starters of that type have reduced stats.
